text_blocks.text: At the centre of this wing is the famous
balcony with the Centre Room behind its glass doors.
This is a Chinese-style saloon enhanced by Queen Mary,
who, working with the designer Sir Charles Allom,
created a more "binding" Chinese theme in the late
1920s, although the lacquer doors were brought from
Brighton in 1873. Running the length of the piano
nobile of the east wing is the great gallery, modestly
known as the Principal Corridor, which runs the length
of the eastern side of the quadrangle. It has mirrored
doors, and mirrored cross walls reflecting porcelain
pagodas and other oriental furniture from Brighton. The
Chinese Luncheon Room and Yellow Drawing Room are
situated at each end of this gallery, with the Centre
Room obviously placed in the centre.
